metadata.title: Economics and Computation Exam Quiz Data
metadata.study_mode: challenging active recall and exam-style discrimination

questions.prompt: A direct-revelation mechanism is written as \(M=(x,p)\). Which interpretation is correct?
choices: \(x\) maps reported types to feasible outcomes, while \(p_i\) maps the whole report profile to agent \(i\)'s payment.
\(x\) maps true types to payments, while \(p\) maps bids to utilities.
\(x\) is a feasible outcome and \(p\) is a scalar reserve price.
\(x\) maps each agent's report independently to an allocation, while \(p_i\) depends only on \(b_i\).
questions.answer: \(x\) maps reported types to feasible outcomes, while \(p_i\) maps the whole report profile to agent \(i\)'s payment.
questions.explanation: Both allocation and payment rules can depend on the full report profile.

questions.prompt: In \(x(\mathbf b)\in\arg\max_{y\in X}\sum_i b_i(y)\), what is \(X\)?
choices: The set of feasible outcomes or allocations.
The set of truthful bid profiles.
questions.answer: The set of feasible outcomes or allocations.
questions.explanation: The rule \(x(\cdot)\) is a function; \(x(\mathbf b)\) is one selected element of \(X\).

questions.prompt: For quasi-linear utility \(u_i(\mathbf b;v_i)=v_i(x(\mathbf b))-p_i(\mathbf b)\), which quantity is private?
questions.answer: The true valuation function \(v_i\).
questions.explanation: The mechanism rules are public; agents' valuations are private information.

questions.prompt: Which inequality is the DSIC condition for agent \(i\)?
questions.explanation: Truthful reporting must maximize utility for every fixed report profile of the other agents.

questions.prompt: In a single-item second-price auction with bids \((12,9,4)\), what are the winner's payment and utility if her value is \(12\)?
questions.answer: Payment \(9\), utility \(3\).
questions.explanation: The highest bidder wins and pays the second-highest bid.

questions.prompt: Why is bidding above one's value weakly dominated in a second-price auction?
choices: It can turn a loss into a win at a price above value, while it cannot create a beneficial outcome unavailable under truthful bidding.
It always increases the payment even when the bidder loses.
It causes the bidder to pay her own bid.
It changes the second-highest bid into the reserve price.
questions.answer: It can turn a loss into a win at a price above value, while it cannot create a beneficial outcome unavailable under truthful bidding.
questions.explanation: The dangerous case is when the rival's highest bid lies strictly between the true value and the overbid.

questions.prompt: For a \(k\)-unit auction with unit-demand bidders and identical units, which DSIC rule generalizes the Vickrey auction?
choices: Allocate to the \(k\) highest bidders and charge each winner the \((k+1)\)-st highest bid.
Allocate to the \(k\) highest bidders and charge each her own bid.
Allocate to the \((k+1)\) highest bidders and charge the \(k\)-th bid.
Allocate randomly among the top \(k+1\) bidders and charge zero.
questions.answer: Allocate to the \(k\) highest bidders and charge each winner the \((k+1)\)-st highest bid.
questions.explanation: Each winner faces the same critical threshold: the highest losing bid.

questions.prompt: A seller has production cost \(c\). Which modification of the second-price auction is efficient and DSIC?
choices: Sell only if the highest bid is at least \(c\), and charge the winner \(\max\{c,b_{(2)}\}\).
Sell only if the second-highest bid is at least \(c\), and charge \(b_{(1)}\).
Sell only if the highest bid is at least \(c\), and charge \(c+b_{(2)}\).
questions.answer: Sell only if the highest bid is at least \(c\), and charge the winner \(\max\{c,b_{(2)}\}\).
questions.explanation: The winner pays the critical value needed both to beat competitors and to cover production cost.

questions.prompt: In a procurement auction where contractor \(i\) has private cost \(c_i\), which rule is the reverse-Vickrey analogue?
choices: Choose the lowest reported cost and pay the second-lowest reported cost.
Choose the lowest reported cost and pay that report.
Choose the second-lowest reported cost and pay the lowest report.
Choose the highest reported cost and pay the second-highest report.
questions.answer: Choose the lowest reported cost and pay the second-lowest reported cost.
questions.explanation: The winner is paid her critical cost threshold.

questions.prompt: For a single-parameter allocation rule \(x_i(b_i,b_{-i})\in\{0,1\}\), monotonicity means:
choices: If \(i\) wins at \(b_i\), then she also wins at every \(b_i'>b_i\), holding \(b_{-i}\) fixed.
If \(i\) wins at \(b_i\), her payment strictly increases with every larger bid.
If \(i\) loses at \(b_i\), she also loses at every larger bid.
The total number of winners is nondecreasing in every bid.
questions.answer: If \(i\) wins at \(b_i\), then she also wins at every \(b_i'>b_i\), holding \(b_{-i}\) fixed.
questions.explanation: This is the implementability condition in deterministic single-parameter environments.

questions.prompt: For a monotone binary allocation rule, the DSIC payment of a winning agent is:
choices: Her critical bid, i.e. the smallest bid at which she would still win.
The largest losing bid among all agents, regardless of feasibility.
Any payment independent of her own report.
questions.answer: Her critical bid, i.e. the smallest bid at which she would still win.
questions.explanation: Critical-value payments make truthful bidding a dominant strategy.

questions.prompt: Which statement best captures Myerson's lemma in a single-parameter environment?
choices: An allocation rule is implementable in DSIC iff it is monotone; when implementable, the normalized payment rule is essentially unique.
Every welfare-maximizing allocation is polynomial-time computable.
DSIC is equivalent to budget balance.
questions.answer: An allocation rule is implementable in DSIC iff it is monotone; when implementable, the normalized payment rule is essentially unique.
questions.explanation: The payment identity converts monotonicity into truthfulness.

questions.prompt: For fractional single-parameter allocation \(x_i(z,b_{-i})\in[0,1]\), the normalized payment identity is:
questions.explanation: This is the standard payment formula with zero payment at bid zero.

questions.prompt: Which claim about a first-price auction is correct?
choices: Truthful bidding is generally not a dominant strategy because a winner can often shade her bid and still win.
It is DSIC because the highest value always wins.
It is equivalent to the second-price auction pointwise in payments.
questions.answer: Truthful bidding is generally not a dominant strategy because a winner can often shade her bid and still win.
questions.explanation: Efficiency of an outcome under truthful bids does not imply truthful bidding is optimal.

questions.prompt: In an English ascending auction with sufficiently small increments, the final price approximates:
choices: The seller's value plus the highest valuation.
questions.answer: The second-highest valuation.
questions.explanation: The highest-value bidder remains after the second-highest bidder drops out.

questions.prompt: In the knapsack auction, bidder \(i\) has public size \(w_i\) and private value \(v_i\). Which feasibility constraint is correct?
questions.answer: \(\sum_i w_i x_i\le W\), with \(x_i\in\{0,1\}\).
questions.explanation: The public sizes consume the shared capacity \(W\).

questions.prompt: Why can exact welfare maximization be unsuitable as an allocation rule in a knapsack auction?
choices: Computing the welfare-maximizing feasible subset is NP-hard.
It cannot be paired with payments.
questions.answer: Computing the welfare-maximizing feasible subset is NP-hard.
questions.explanation: The mechanism-design challenge is to combine truthfulness, approximation, and polynomial running time.

questions.prompt: Which compromise is central to algorithmic mechanism design for NP-hard welfare problems?
choices: Replace exact welfare maximization by a polynomial-time monotone approximation algorithm.
Keep exact welfare maximization but drop all payments.
Use any approximation algorithm and assume it is truthful.
questions.answer: Replace exact welfare maximization by a polynomial-time monotone approximation algorithm.
questions.explanation: Monotonicity is essential for DSIC in single-parameter settings.

questions.prompt: A greedy knapsack rule orders bidders by decreasing \(b_i/w_i\) and packs while feasible. Why is a comparison with the highest-value single item often added?
choices: To obtain a constant-factor guarantee when one large item carries much of the optimum value.
To eliminate the need for payments.
To convert private sizes into public sizes.
questions.answer: To obtain a constant-factor guarantee when one large item carries much of the optimum value.
questions.explanation: Density-greedy alone can miss a single very valuable item.

questions.prompt: The standard greedy-plus-best-single-item knapsack heuristic guarantees at least:
questions.answer: \(\frac12\) of optimal welfare.
questions.explanation: The fractional optimum is bounded by the greedy prefix plus at most one additional item.

questions.prompt: Which argument proves monotonicity of density-based greedy knapsack when sizes are public?
choices: Increasing \(b_i\) only moves item \(i\) earlier in the density order, so it cannot lose solely because of the increase, with fixed tie-breaking.
The critical payment forces the allocation to be monotone.
questions.answer: Increasing \(b_i\) only moves item \(i\) earlier in the density order, so it cannot lose solely because of the increase, with fixed tie-breaking.
questions.explanation: Fixed, bid-independent tie-breaking is important.

questions.prompt: Why does applying a generic knapsack FPTAS not automatically yield a truthful mechanism?
choices: Its allocation rule may fail monotonicity as a bid changes.
FPTAS algorithms cannot run in polynomial time.
Payments cannot be computed for exact algorithms.
questions.answer: Its allocation rule may fail monotonicity as a bid changes.
questions.explanation: Approximation quality alone says nothing about strategic implementability.

questions.prompt: For a selected bidder under a monotone knapsack allocation, the DSIC payment is best described as:
choices: The infimum bid at which that bidder remains selected, fixing the others' bids.
The bidder's reported bid minus the approximation error.
questions.answer: The infimum bid at which that bidder remains selected, fixing the others' bids.
questions.explanation: This is the critical bid induced by the allocation algorithm.

questions.prompt: Which statement is the Revelation Principle for dominant strategies?
choices: Any mechanism in which every agent has a dominant strategy has an outcome-equivalent direct-revelation DSIC mechanism.
Every Bayesian mechanism has a dominant-strategy implementation.
questions.answer: Any mechanism in which every agent has a dominant strategy has an outcome-equivalent direct-revelation DSIC mechanism.
questions.explanation: The direct mechanism simulates the dominant strategies agents would have played in the original mechanism.

questions.prompt: In the proof of the Revelation Principle, the direct mechanism receiving type report \(\hat v_i\) submits what to the original mechanism?
choices: The dominant strategy \(s_i(\hat v_i)\) associated with that reported type.
questions.answer: The dominant strategy \(s_i(\hat v_i)\) associated with that reported type.
questions.explanation: Truthful reporting is optimal because it triggers the original dominant strategy for the true type.

questions.prompt: Suppose an approximation rule has excellent welfare but is nonmonotone. What follows in a deterministic single-parameter environment?
choices: No payment rule can make that allocation rule DSIC.
It is DSIC if the approximation ratio exceeds \(1/2\).
It becomes DSIC after adding a reserve price.
questions.answer: No payment rule can make that allocation rule DSIC.
questions.explanation: This is the necessity direction of Myerson's lemma.

questions.prompt: Why must tie-breaking in a monotone allocation algorithm be fixed independently of bids?
choices: Bid-dependent tie-breaking can make a bidder lose after increasing her bid.
It is required for polynomial running time.
questions.answer: Bid-dependent tie-breaking can make a bidder lose after increasing her bid.
questions.explanation: Strategic monotonicity can be destroyed by seemingly harmless tie-breaking.

questions.prompt: What distinguishes a multi-parameter environment from a single-parameter one?
choices: An agent may have different private values for many outcomes or bundles, not merely one scalar value multiplying a public allocation quantity.
The feasible set contains multiple outcomes.
questions.answer: An agent may have different private values for many outcomes or bundles, not merely one scalar value multiplying a public allocation quantity.
questions.explanation: Combinatorial auctions are the canonical example.

questions.prompt: In a combinatorial auction with item set \(M\), bidder \(i\) reports:
choices: A cost function on edges of a network.
questions.answer: A valuation function \(b_i:2^M\to\mathbb R_{\ge0}\).
questions.explanation: Each subset of items can have a distinct reported value.

questions.prompt: The VCG allocation rule chooses:
questions.answer: \(x(\mathbf b)\in\arg\max_{x\in X}\sum_i b_i(x)\).
questions.explanation: VCG chooses an outcome maximizing reported social welfare.

questions.prompt: Under Clarke pivot payments, agent \(i\) pays:
questions.answer: \(p_i(\mathbf b)=\max_{x\in X}\sum_{j\ne i}b_j(x)-\sum_{j\ne i}b_j(x(\mathbf b))\).
questions.explanation: The payment is the externality agent \(i\) imposes on the others.

questions.prompt: What is the 'externality' in a VCG mechanism?
choices: The decrease in maximum achievable welfare of the other agents caused by agent \(i\)'s presence and selected allocation.
The total revenue collected from all agents.
The difference between first and second price in every environment.
questions.answer: The decrease in maximum achievable welfare of the other agents caused by agent \(i\)'s presence and selected allocation.
questions.explanation: Second-price payments are a special case of this general expression.

questions.prompt: Why is truthful reporting dominant in VCG?
choices: Agent \(i\)'s utility equals total reported welfare of the chosen outcome, with \(i\) evaluated at her true value, minus a term independent of her report.
Every outcome gives the same utility.
questions.answer: Agent \(i\)'s utility equals total reported welfare of the chosen outcome, with \(i\) evaluated at her true value, minus a term independent of her report.
questions.explanation: Truthful reporting aligns the allocation objective with the agent's utility objective.

questions.prompt: Which statement about VCG computational efficiency is correct?
choices: VCG is computationally efficient only when the welfare-maximization problem and required counterfactual optimizations can be solved efficiently.
VCG is always polynomial time because it is DSIC.
VCG avoids solving any optimization problem.
VCG is efficient only in single-item auctions.
questions.answer: VCG is computationally efficient only when the welfare-maximization problem and required counterfactual optimizations can be solved efficiently.
questions.explanation: Truthfulness does not remove computational hardness.

questions.prompt: Which is a genuine practical limitation of combinatorial VCG auctions?
choices: Preference elicitation may require reporting values for exponentially many bundles.
questions.answer: Preference elicitation may require reporting values for exponentially many bundles.
questions.explanation: There are \(2^{|M|}\) possible bundles.

questions.prompt: VCG revenue can be low because:
choices: Payments reflect externalities, which can be small even when winners have high values.
VCG charges every winner her full value.
VCG maximizes revenue rather than welfare.
questions.answer: Payments reflect externalities, which can be small even when winners have high values.
questions.explanation: VCG optimizes welfare, not seller revenue.

questions.prompt: For a single-item auction, the VCG payment of the winner reduces to:
choices: The highest bid among the other bidders.
The sum of all losing bids.
questions.answer: The highest bid among the other bidders.
questions.explanation: Removing the winner lets the best losing bidder obtain the item.

questions.prompt: For a bipartite assignment problem, computing VCG payments generally requires:
choices: The optimal matching with all agents and counterfactual optimal matchings with each paying agent removed.
questions.answer: The optimal matching with all agents and counterfactual optimal matchings with each paying agent removed.
questions.explanation: Each externality is a difference between two optimal welfare values.

questions.prompt: If an agent's presence does not change the welfare attainable by the other agents, her Clarke pivot payment is:
questions.answer: Zero.
questions.explanation: The agent imposes no externality.

questions.prompt: Which property is not guaranteed by VCG in general?
questions.answer: High or optimal revenue.
questions.explanation: VCG is a welfare mechanism, and revenue can be arbitrarily poor.

questions.prompt: In a combinatorial auction, why can a bidder's VCG payment differ sharply from any single losing bid?
choices: The relevant counterfactual is the best reallocation of all items among all other bidders, not one runner-up bid.
The payment equals the average losing bid.
questions.answer: The relevant counterfactual is the best reallocation of all items among all other bidders, not one runner-up bid.
questions.explanation: Externalities are global in multi-item allocation problems.

questions.prompt: Let \(W_{-i}^*\) be the maximum welfare achievable by agents other than \(i\), and let \(W_{-i}(x^*)\) be their welfare in the chosen allocation. The pivot payment is:
questions.explanation: It is the harm to others due to accommodating agent \(i\).

questions.prompt: For one bidder with value \(v\) and posted price \(r\), seller revenue is:
questions.answer: \(r\) if \(v\ge r\), and \(0\) otherwise.
questions.explanation: A posted price is a take-it-or-leave-it DSIC mechanism.

questions.prompt: Why is a Bayesian model introduced for revenue maximization?
choices: Different auctions perform best on different valuation profiles, so distributions provide a criterion for comparing expected revenue.
Welfare cannot be defined ex post.
DSIC requires bidders to know each other's values.
questions.answer: Different auctions perform best on different valuation profiles, so distributions provide a criterion for comparing expected revenue.
questions.explanation: Unlike welfare maximization, there is generally no input-independent ex-post optimal DSIC auction for revenue.

questions.prompt: The virtual valuation for bidder \(i\) is:
questions.answer: \(\phi_i(v)=v-\frac{1-F_i(v)}{f_i(v)}\).
questions.explanation: Virtual values transform expected payments into expected virtual surplus.

questions.prompt: Myerson's revenue identity states, for a DSIC single-parameter auction under standard normalization:
choices: \(\mathbb E[\sum_i p_i(v)]=\mathbb E[\max_i v_i]\) for every auction.
questions.answer: \(\mathbb E[\sum_i p_i(v)]=\mathbb E[\sum_i \phi_i(v_i)x_i(v)]\).
questions.explanation: Expected revenue equals expected virtual welfare, not necessarily pointwise.

questions.prompt: A distribution is regular when:
choices: Its virtual valuation function is nondecreasing.
questions.answer: Its virtual valuation function is nondecreasing.
questions.explanation: Regularity makes virtual-welfare maximization monotone and hence implementable.

questions.prompt: For i.i.d. regular bidders in a single-item auction, the optimal auction is:
choices: A Vickrey auction with reserve price \(r=\phi^{-1}(0)\).
A random allocation with posted prices.
A Vickrey auction with reserve equal to the mean value.
questions.answer: A Vickrey auction with reserve price \(r=\phi^{-1}(0)\).
questions.explanation: The item is allocated only when the highest nonnegative virtual value is positive.

questions.prompt: For non-identically distributed regular bidders, the revenue-optimal allocation can award the item to someone other than the highest-value bidder because:
choices: Bidders are ranked by virtual values, which use bidder-specific distributions.
Payments determine the allocation after bids are submitted.
The highest bid is ignored whenever there is a reserve.
questions.answer: Bidders are ranked by virtual values, which use bidder-specific distributions.
questions.explanation: A lower value from a favorable distribution can have a higher virtual value.

questions.prompt: For \(V\sim U[0,1]\), the virtual valuation is:
questions.explanation: Here \(F(v)=v\) and \(f(v)=1\).

questions.prompt: For \(V\sim U[0,1]\), the monopoly reserve is:
questions.explanation: Solve \(\phi(r)=2r-1=0\).

questions.prompt: The prophet inequality in the lecture guarantees a threshold strategy with expected reward at least:
questions.answer: \(\frac12\,\mathbb E[\max_i \pi_i]\).
questions.explanation: The decision maker sees prizes sequentially and accepts at most one.

questions.prompt: A key feature of the prophet-inequality strategy used in simple auctions is:
choices: A single threshold chosen from the known distributions before observing realizations.
Choosing the largest realized prize after seeing all of them.
A bidder-specific payment equal to her virtual value.
questions.answer: A single threshold chosen from the known distributions before observing realizations.
questions.explanation: The threshold trades off the probability of accepting against the value conditional on acceptance.

questions.prompt: The Bulow-Klemperer theorem compares which two auctions for i.i.d. regular bidders?
choices: The Vickrey auction with \(n+1\) bidders earns at least the optimal auction with \(n\) bidders.
The first-price auction with \(n\) bidders earns exactly the Vickrey auction with \(n\) bidders.
The optimal auction with \(n+1\) bidders earns less than Vickrey with \(n\) bidders.
A posted price with one bidder dominates every auction with two bidders.
questions.answer: The Vickrey auction with \(n+1\) bidders earns at least the optimal auction with \(n\) bidders.
questions.explanation: Competition can substitute for detailed distributional optimization.

questions.prompt: What design lesson is suggested by Bulow-Klemperer?
choices: Increasing competition may be more valuable and robust than fine-tuning a complicated optimal auction.
Revenue maximization and welfare maximization coincide pointwise.
questions.answer: Increasing competition may be more valuable and robust than fine-tuning a complicated optimal auction.
questions.explanation: One extra bidder can beat optimal design with one fewer bidder.

questions.prompt: Why are simple near-optimal auctions studied after Myerson's exact characterization?
choices: Exact optimal auctions can be complex, distribution-sensitive, and difficult to explain or implement.
Exact optimization never uses reserve prices.
Simple auctions always maximize revenue exactly.
questions.answer: Exact optimal auctions can be complex, distribution-sensitive, and difficult to explain or implement.
questions.explanation: Approximation is used here to recover simplicity and robustness.

questions.prompt: Which statement correctly distinguishes welfare and revenue objectives?
choices: Welfare-maximizing DSIC mechanisms can often be optimal ex post, whereas revenue optimization generally requires a distributional criterion.
Revenue always equals welfare in DSIC mechanisms.
Welfare maximization requires Bayesian priors, but revenue does not.
VCG maximizes expected revenue for all distributions.
questions.answer: Welfare-maximizing DSIC mechanisms can often be optimal ex post, whereas revenue optimization generally requires a distributional criterion.
questions.explanation: This is why welfare is unusually tractable from an incentive perspective.

questions.prompt: A normal-form game consists of:
choices: Players \(N\), strategy sets \(S_i\), and payoff functions \(u_i:\prod_jS_j\to\mathbb R\).
Only a payoff matrix and one strategy set.
A feasible allocation set and payment rules.
A graph and edge latency functions only.
questions.answer: Players \(N\), strategy sets \(S_i\), and payoff functions \(u_i:\prod_jS_j\to\mathbb R\).
questions.explanation: A state is a complete strategy profile.

questions.prompt: A strategy \(s_i\) is dominant for player \(i\) if:
choices: For every \(s_{-i}\) and every alternative \(s_i'\), \(u_i(s_i,s_{-i})\ge u_i(s_i',s_{-i})\).
It is a best response to at least one profile \(s_{-i}\).
Every other player uses the same strategy.
It appears in some Nash equilibrium.
questions.answer: For every \(s_{-i}\) and every alternative \(s_i'\), \(u_i(s_i,s_{-i})\ge u_i(s_i',s_{-i})\).
questions.explanation: Dominance is uniform over all opponents' actions.

questions.prompt: A pure Nash equilibrium \(s\) satisfies:
choices: \(u_i(s_i,s_{-i})\ge u_i(s_i',s_{-i})\) for every player \(i\) and every \(s_i'\).
Every player uses a dominant strategy.
The sum of utilities is maximal.
questions.answer: \(u_i(s_i,s_{-i})\ge u_i(s_i',s_{-i})\) for every player \(i\) and every \(s_i'\).
questions.explanation: No player can profitably deviate unilaterally.

questions.prompt: Which implication is valid?
choices: Every dominant-strategy equilibrium is a Nash equilibrium, but not every Nash equilibrium is a dominant-strategy equilibrium.
A game with a Nash equilibrium must have a dominant strategy for every player.
Dominant strategies exist only in zero-sum games.
questions.answer: Every dominant-strategy equilibrium is a Nash equilibrium, but not every Nash equilibrium is a dominant-strategy equilibrium.
questions.explanation: Dominance is a stronger requirement than best response at one profile.

questions.prompt: Nash's theorem guarantees that every finite game has:
choices: At least one mixed Nash equilibrium.
At least one pure Nash equilibrium.
questions.answer: At least one mixed Nash equilibrium.
questions.explanation: Pure equilibria need not exist, as Rock-Paper-Scissors shows.

questions.prompt: The support of a mixed strategy \(p_i\) is:
choices: The set of pure strategies assigned positive probability.
The set of best responses of the opponents.
The set of all pure strategies in the game.
questions.answer: The set of pure strategies assigned positive probability.
questions.explanation: Strategies outside the support have probability zero.

questions.prompt: In a mixed Nash equilibrium, every pure strategy in a player's support must:
choices: Yield the same maximal expected payoff against the opponents' mixed strategies.
Minimize the opponent's payoff in every game.
questions.answer: Yield the same maximal expected payoff against the opponents' mixed strategies.
questions.explanation: Otherwise probability could be shifted from a worse supported action to a better one.

questions.prompt: A strictly dominated pure strategy:
choices: Cannot receive positive probability in any Nash equilibrium.
Must be played in every mixed equilibrium.
Can be a best response to every profile.
Can be removed only in zero-sum games.
questions.answer: Cannot receive positive probability in any Nash equilibrium.
questions.explanation: A strictly better alternative exists against every opponents' profile.

questions.prompt: A zero-sum game satisfies:
choices: \(u_1(s)+u_2(s)=0\) (or another fixed constant after normalization) for every state \(s\).
There is always a pure equilibrium.
questions.answer: \(u_1(s)+u_2(s)=0\) (or another fixed constant after normalization) for every state \(s\).
questions.explanation: One player's gain is the other's loss.

questions.prompt: For a two-player zero-sum matrix game with row payoff matrix \(A\), mixed strategies \(x,y\) give row expected payoff:
questions.answer: \(x^TAy\).
questions.explanation: This is the bilinear expectation over independently sampled actions.

questions.prompt: The minimax theorem states:
choices: Every zero-sum game has a pure saddle point.
The value depends on who commits first.
questions.answer: \(\max_x\min_y x^TAy=\min_y\max_x x^TAy\).
questions.explanation: Randomization eliminates the first-mover disadvantage in finite zero-sum games.

questions.prompt: In Prisoner's Dilemma, the socially optimal outcome can differ from the Nash equilibrium because:
choices: Individual incentives under unilateral deviations need not align with maximizing total welfare.
Nash equilibrium always minimizes total utility.
Players cannot observe their own payoffs.
questions.answer: Individual incentives under unilateral deviations need not align with maximizing total welfare.
questions.explanation: Equilibrium is a stability concept, not an efficiency guarantee.

questions.prompt: A best response of player \(i\) to \(s_{-i}\) is:
choices: Any strategy in the support of the opponent.
questions.answer: Any \(s_i\in\arg\max_{a_i\in S_i}u_i(a_i,s_{-i})\).
questions.explanation: Best responses depend on what the others do.

questions.prompt: Why can computing a Nash equilibrium be challenging despite Nash's existence theorem?
choices: Existence is nonconstructive with respect to efficient computation; equilibrium computation can be computationally hard.
Finite games have infinitely many states.
Mixed strategies are not finite objects.
questions.answer: Existence is nonconstructive with respect to efficient computation; equilibrium computation can be computationally hard.
questions.explanation: Existence and polynomial-time computability are distinct questions.

questions.prompt: In a symmetric bandwidth-sharing game, a player's best response is found by:
choices: Maximizing her utility with respect to her own action while holding all other actions fixed.
Maximizing the sum of all utilities.
Assuming all opponents copy her action after she moves.
questions.answer: Maximizing her utility with respect to her own action while holding all other actions fixed.
questions.explanation: This is the unilateral-deviation viewpoint underlying Nash equilibrium.

questions.prompt: In nonatomic selfish routing, a feasible flow is at Wardrop equilibrium when:
choices: Every used path has minimum latency among all paths available to the same commodity.
questions.answer: Every used path has minimum latency among all paths available to the same commodity.
questions.explanation: An infinitesimal user cannot improve by switching paths.

questions.prompt: The social cost of a flow \(f\) with edge latency functions \(c_e\) is:
questions.answer: \(C(f)=\sum_e f_ec_e(f_e)\).
questions.explanation: Each edge's latency is incurred by all flow using that edge.

questions.prompt: The Price of Anarchy for a cost-minimization game is typically:
questions.answer: \(\frac{\text{cost of worst equilibrium}}{\text{cost of optimum}}\).
questions.explanation: PoA measures inefficiency caused by decentralized selfish behavior.

questions.prompt: In Pigou's network with one unit of demand, one edge of constant latency \(1\), and one edge of latency \(x\), the Wardrop equilibrium sends:
choices: Half the flow on each edge.
All flow on the constant edge.
questions.answer: All flow on the \(x\) edge.
questions.explanation: At full load the variable edge has latency \(1\), so no infinitesimal user can strictly improve.

questions.prompt: For the same Pigou network, the socially optimal split is:
choices: All flow on the constant edge.
\(2/3\) on the \(x\) edge and \(1/3\) on the constant edge.
questions.answer: \(1/2\) on each edge.
questions.explanation: Minimize \(x^2+(1-x)\) over the variable-edge flow \(x\).

questions.prompt: The Price of Anarchy in the linear Pigou example is:
questions.explanation: Equilibrium cost is \(1\); optimum cost is \(3/4\).

questions.prompt: Braess's paradox shows that:
choices: Adding an edge can worsen equilibrium travel time even though the feasible set expands.
Removing an edge always worsens every user's travel time.
Every equilibrium is socially optimal in series-parallel networks.
More capacity always lowers equilibrium cost.
questions.answer: Adding an edge can worsen equilibrium travel time even though the feasible set expands.
questions.explanation: Strategic route choices can turn extra capacity into a harmful option.

questions.prompt: Why can nonlinear latency functions lead to unbounded PoA over unrestricted classes?
choices: They can create arbitrarily large gaps between marginal social cost and private latency.
questions.answer: They can create arbitrarily large gaps between marginal social cost and private latency.
questions.explanation: The Pigou construction can be amplified using steep cost functions.

questions.prompt: A system-optimal flow for separable latency functions can be characterized as a Wardrop equilibrium under which modified edge costs?
questions.answer: Marginal costs \(c_e(x)+x c_e'(x)\).
questions.explanation: Each additional unit creates congestion externality \(x c_e'(x)\).

questions.prompt: Pigouvian tolls that induce the system optimum are:
questions.explanation: Users then perceive marginal social cost.

questions.prompt: A smoothness proof for a cost game typically establishes an inequality of the form:
questions.answer: \(\sum_i C_i(s_i^*,s_{-i})\le \lambda C(s^*)+\mu C(s)\).
questions.explanation: Combined with equilibrium inequalities, this yields \(C(s)\le\lambda/(1-\mu)C(s^*)\).

questions.prompt: If a cost-minimization game is \((\lambda,\mu)\)-smooth with \(\mu<1\), the standard PoA bound is:
questions.answer: \(\lambda/(1-\mu)\).
questions.explanation: Rearrange \((1-\mu)C(s)\le\lambda C(s^*)\).

questions.prompt: A major robustness advantage of smoothness bounds is that they extend beyond pure Nash equilibria to:
choices: Coarse correlated equilibria and outcomes of no-regret learning.
questions.answer: Coarse correlated equilibria and outcomes of no-regret learning.
questions.explanation: Smoothness uses inequalities preserved under expectation.

questions.prompt: In atomic selfish routing, the main difference from nonatomic routing is:
choices: Each player controls a non-negligible packet or demand and can affect edge loads by a unilateral deviation.
questions.answer: Each player controls a non-negligible packet or demand and can affect edge loads by a unilateral deviation.
questions.explanation: The infinitesimal-user Wardrop condition no longer directly applies.

questions.prompt: Which statement about equilibrium and optimum is correct?
choices: An equilibrium minimizes each user's unilateral incentive, while an optimum minimizes aggregate cost; they need not coincide.
Every equilibrium globally minimizes aggregate cost.
Every optimum is necessarily a dominant-strategy equilibrium.
questions.answer: An equilibrium minimizes each user's unilateral incentive, while an optimum minimizes aggregate cost; they need not coincide.
questions.explanation: The distinction drives the study of PoA.

questions.prompt: An exact potential function \(\Phi\) for a cost game satisfies:
choices: \(\Phi\) equals social cost in every game.
questions.answer: \(C_i(s_i',s_{-i})-C_i(s)=\Phi(s_i',s_{-i})-\Phi(s)\) for every unilateral deviation.
questions.explanation: Individual improvement is exactly tracked by potential change.

questions.prompt: Why does every finite exact potential game have a pure Nash equilibrium?
choices: A global minimum of the potential admits no unilateral cost-decreasing deviation.
Nash's mixed-equilibrium theorem directly implies a pure equilibrium.
The social optimum always minimizes every player's cost.
questions.answer: A global minimum of the potential admits no unilateral cost-decreasing deviation.
questions.explanation: Finiteness ensures the potential attains a minimum.

questions.prompt: Best-response dynamics in a finite potential game terminates when:
choices: Every improving move strictly decreases a finite-valued potential over a finite state space.
questions.answer: Every improving move strictly decreases a finite-valued potential over a finite state space.
questions.explanation: No state can repeat along a strict potential descent path.

questions.prompt: Rosenthal's potential for an atomic congestion game is:
questions.answer: \(\Phi(s)=\sum_e\sum_{j=1}^{n_e(s)}c_e(j)\).
questions.explanation: Adding one user to an edge increases potential by exactly that user's new edge cost.

questions.prompt: Which inclusion among equilibrium concepts is correct?
choices: Pure NE \(\subseteq\) mixed NE \(\subseteq\) correlated equilibrium \(\subseteq\) coarse correlated equilibrium.
CCE \(\subseteq\) CE \(\subseteq\) mixed NE \(\subseteq\) pure NE.
questions.answer: Pure NE \(\subseteq\) mixed NE \(\subseteq\) correlated equilibrium \(\subseteq\) coarse correlated equilibrium.
questions.explanation: Each successive concept permits a wider class of outcome distributions.

questions.prompt: A correlated equilibrium allows a mediator to:
choices: Draw a joint action profile and privately recommend each player's component, with no profitable deviation conditional on the recommendation.
Reveal all private recommendations publicly before play.
questions.answer: Draw a joint action profile and privately recommend each player's component, with no profitable deviation conditional on the recommendation.
questions.explanation: Players may condition deviations on the recommendation they receive.

questions.prompt: A coarse correlated equilibrium differs from a correlated equilibrium because deviations in CCE are:
choices: Chosen unconditionally before seeing any recommendation or realized action.
Allowed to depend on the recommendation.
questions.answer: Chosen unconditionally before seeing any recommendation or realized action.
questions.explanation: CCE protects against fixed unilateral deviations.

questions.prompt: For a cost game, distribution \(\sigma\) is a CCE if for all \(i\) and fixed deviations \(s_i'\):
questions.answer: \(\mathbb E_{s\sim\sigma}[C_i(s)]\le \mathbb E_{s\sim\sigma}[C_i(s_i',s_{-i})]\).
questions.explanation: No fixed action would have reduced expected cost.

questions.prompt: Which distributions correspond exactly to mixed Nash equilibria within the set of correlated equilibria?
choices: Product distributions satisfying the equilibrium inequalities.
Distributions supported on one outcome only.
questions.answer: Product distributions satisfying the equilibrium inequalities.
questions.explanation: Mixed Nash assumes independent randomization across players.

questions.prompt: Why are CE and CCE often considered computationally easier than Nash equilibrium?
choices: Their defining incentive constraints are linear in the joint distribution, enabling linear programming or learning-based computation.
They always consist of one pure outcome.
questions.answer: Their defining incentive constraints are linear in the joint distribution, enabling linear programming or learning-based computation.
questions.explanation: Nash equilibrium imposes nonconvex product constraints.

questions.prompt: A local minimum of an exact potential function corresponds to:
questions.answer: A pure Nash equilibrium.
questions.explanation: No unilateral move lowers the potential, hence none lowers the deviator's cost.

questions.prompt: Which claim is false?
choices: Every pure Nash equilibrium minimizes the potential globally.
Every global potential minimum is a pure Nash equilibrium.
Finite potential games admit pure Nash equilibria.
Improvement paths cannot cycle when the potential strictly improves.
questions.answer: Every pure Nash equilibrium minimizes the potential globally.
questions.explanation: A pure equilibrium can be only a local minimum of the potential.

questions.prompt: External regret after \(T\) rounds compares the algorithm's cumulative cost with:
choices: The cumulative cost of the best fixed action in hindsight.
The best action chosen separately on every round.
The social optimum of a game.
The worst fixed action in hindsight.
questions.answer: The cumulative cost of the best fixed action in hindsight.
questions.explanation: The comparator is one constant action used on all rounds.

questions.prompt: An online algorithm has no external regret if:
choices: Its loss is always below every action's loss on every round.
questions.answer: \(R_T/T\to0\) as \(T\to\infty\).
questions.explanation: Average performance approaches that of the best fixed action.

questions.prompt: The multiplicative weights algorithm chooses action \(a\) with probability proportional to:
choices: The inverse of the number of actions.
questions.answer: Its current weight.
questions.explanation: Weights encode action credibility based on past performance.

questions.prompt: Under multiplicative weights for costs, an action with high observed cost should have its weight:
choices: Reduced multiplicatively more than an action with low cost.
Set permanently to zero after one loss.
questions.answer: Reduced multiplicatively more than an action with low cost.
questions.explanation: Poor actions become less likely in future rounds.

questions.prompt: A typical multiplicative-weights regret bound has order:
questions.answer: \(O(\sqrt{T\ln n})\) for \(n\) actions.
questions.explanation: Thus average regret is \(O(\sqrt{\ln n/T})\).

questions.prompt: If every player in a repeated game has vanishing external regret, the empirical distribution of play converges toward:
choices: The set of coarse correlated equilibria.
The set of pure Nash equilibria only.
questions.answer: The set of coarse correlated equilibria.
questions.explanation: External-regret inequalities become the CCE inequalities for empirical play.

questions.prompt: If every player has vanishing swap regret, the empirical distribution of play converges toward:
questions.answer: The set of correlated equilibria.
questions.explanation: Swap regret allows deviations that replace each recommended action by another action.

questions.prompt: Swap regret compares actual play against:
choices: Every switching function mapping each played action to an alternative action.
Only the best single fixed action.
questions.answer: Every switching function mapping each played action to an alternative action.
questions.explanation: It is stronger than external regret.

questions.prompt: Which implication is correct?
choices: No swap regret implies no external regret, but not conversely in general.
No external regret implies no swap regret.
They are identical for every action set.
Neither has any relation to equilibrium.
questions.answer: No swap regret implies no external regret, but not conversely in general.
questions.explanation: Constant switching functions are special cases of swap deviations.

questions.prompt: The standard reduction from swap regret to external regret runs:
choices: One external-regret algorithm for each source action and combines their recommendations through a stationary distribution.
One algorithm for each player profile.
A VCG mechanism on every round.
questions.answer: One external-regret algorithm for each source action and combines their recommendations through a stationary distribution.
questions.explanation: The internal algorithms learn where to switch from each action.

questions.prompt: How can no-regret learning prove the minimax theorem?
choices: Running no-regret algorithms for both players yields time-average strategies whose saddle-point gap vanishes.
No-regret guarantees a pure equilibrium in every zero-sum game.
The row player learns the opponent's private randomness exactly.
Regret makes payoffs identical on every round.
questions.answer: Running no-regret algorithms for both players yields time-average strategies whose saddle-point gap vanishes.
questions.explanation: Average play simultaneously approaches max-min and min-max guarantees.

questions.prompt: In a zero-sum game, if row and column each have average regret at most \(\epsilon\), their empirical mixed strategies form approximately:
choices: A correlated equilibrium with zero value necessarily.
questions.answer: A \(2\epsilon\)-saddle point (up to convention-dependent constants).
questions.explanation: Each regret term contributes to the primal-dual gap.

questions.prompt: An adaptive adversary in online learning may choose the next cost vector based on:
choices: The algorithm's past behavior and current mixed strategy, but not the current random action before committing the cost vector under the standard non-anticipating model.
Future random bits of the learner.
Only a fixed sequence chosen before round one.
The best fixed action in hindsight.
questions.answer: The algorithm's past behavior and current mixed strategy, but not the current random action before committing the cost vector under the standard non-anticipating model.
questions.explanation: This preserves meaningful regret guarantees.

questions.prompt: Why does a no-regret guarantee not imply that the last action profile is a Nash equilibrium?
choices: Regret controls time averages, while individual rounds can keep cycling.
Nash equilibria do not exist in finite games.
Regret is defined only for cooperative games.
No-regret algorithms always converge to dominant strategies.
questions.answer: Regret controls time averages, while individual rounds can keep cycling.
questions.explanation: Rock-Paper-Scissors can cycle while empirical frequencies converge.

questions.prompt: Smoothness plus no-regret learning implies:
choices: The time-average social cost is bounded by the same robust PoA guarantee, up to vanishing regret terms.
All players eventually use pure dominant strategies.
questions.answer: The time-average social cost is bounded by the same robust PoA guarantee, up to vanishing regret terms.
questions.explanation: Empirical play approaches CCE, and smoothness bounds CCE inefficiency.

questions.prompt: In the house-allocation problem studied with Top Trading Cycles, each agent initially:
choices: Owns one house and has a strict preference ordering over houses.
Reports a single scalar value only.
questions.answer: Owns one house and has a strict preference ordering over houses.
questions.explanation: The initial endowment defines property rights and trading cycles.

questions.prompt: In each round of Top Trading Cycles (TTC):
choices: Each remaining agent points to her favorite remaining house, each remaining house points to its owner, and every directed cycle is executed.
Agents propose to houses that choose their favorite applicant.
The auctioneer maximizes reported monetary welfare.
questions.answer: Each remaining agent points to her favorite remaining house, each remaining house points to its owner, and every directed cycle is executed.
questions.explanation: At least one cycle exists in every finite directed graph with outdegree one.

questions.prompt: Why does TTC terminate?
choices: Every round removes at least one agent-house cycle, hence at least one agent and one house.
Preferences become cardinal after each round.
The number of blocking coalitions increases.
questions.answer: Every round removes at least one agent-house cycle, hence at least one agent and one house.
questions.explanation: With \(n\) agents, there are at most \(n\) elimination rounds.

questions.prompt: A blocking coalition in house allocation is a set of agents that can:
choices: Reallocate their initially owned houses among themselves so everyone is weakly better off and at least one member is strictly better off.
Buy houses from outsiders using transfers.
Change the preference rankings of houses.
questions.answer: Reallocate their initially owned houses among themselves so everyone is weakly better off and at least one member is strictly better off.
questions.explanation: The core excludes allocations vulnerable to such internal trades.

questions.prompt: The core of a house-allocation market consists of:
choices: Allocations maximizing the sum of ranks.
questions.answer: Allocations with no blocking coalition.
questions.explanation: Core stability is defined relative to initial endowments.

questions.prompt: For strict preferences in the Shapley-Scarf housing market, TTC returns:
choices: A random serial dictatorship outcome only.
questions.answer: The unique core allocation.
questions.explanation: TTC successively fixes cycles that every core allocation must respect.

questions.prompt: Which strategic property is associated with TTC under the standard house-allocation model?
choices: Strategyproofness (DSIC): truthful preference reporting is a dominant strategy.
Group strategyproofness for all variants with ties automatically.
questions.answer: Strategyproofness (DSIC): truthful preference reporting is a dominant strategy.
questions.explanation: An agent cannot obtain a more preferred house by misreporting under the standard strict-preference model.

questions.prompt: In kidney exchange, a directed compatibility edge from pair \(i\) to pair \(j\) commonly means:
choices: The donor associated with pair \(i\) can donate to the patient associated with pair \(j\).
Both pairs are incompatible with everyone.
questions.answer: The donor associated with pair \(i\) can donate to the patient associated with pair \(j\).
questions.explanation: A directed cycle corresponds to a feasible exchange among patient-donor pairs.

questions.prompt: Why are long cycles operationally difficult in kidney exchange?
choices: Transplants in a cycle are typically performed simultaneously to prevent reneging, and coordinating many simultaneous surgeries is hard.
Long cycles are mathematically impossible in directed graphs.
questions.answer: Transplants in a cycle are typically performed simultaneously to prevent reneging, and coordinating many simultaneous surgeries is hard.
questions.explanation: This motivates cycle-length constraints.

questions.prompt: If kidney exchange is modeled by an undirected compatibility graph where only pairwise exchanges are allowed, maximizing the number of transplants reduces to:
questions.answer: Finding a maximum-cardinality matching.
questions.explanation: Each matching edge represents one 2-way exchange covering two patient-donor pairs.

questions.prompt: Why can arbitrary tie-breaking among maximum matchings create incentives to hide compatible edges?
choices: An agent may alter which maximum matching is selected and become matched without reducing maximum cardinality.
Removing edges can increase the size of every matching.
Truthful edge reports are always dominant under arbitrary tie-breaking.
questions.answer: An agent may alter which maximum matching is selected and become matched without reducing maximum cardinality.
questions.explanation: Strategic selection among multiple optima matters.

questions.prompt: A fixed priority order used to refine maximum matchings aims to:
choices: Select, among maximum-cardinality matchings, one that lexicographically prioritizes agents according to a report-independent order.
questions.answer: Select, among maximum-cardinality matchings, one that lexicographically prioritizes agents according to a report-independent order.
questions.explanation: Report-independent priorities can remove manipulations caused by arbitrary tie-breaking.

questions.prompt: In the stable matching problem, a pair \((a,h)\) blocks matching \(M\) if:
choices: Applicant \(a\) prefers \(h\) to \(M(a)\) and hospital \(h\) prefers \(a\) to \(M(h)\).
Exactly one side prefers the other.
Both are unmatched in every matching.
They have equal rank under both preference lists.
questions.answer: Applicant \(a\) prefers \(h\) to \(M(a)\) and hospital \(h\) prefers \(a\) to \(M(h)\).
questions.explanation: A stable matching has no mutually beneficial deviation pair.

questions.prompt: In applicant-proposing Deferred Acceptance, when a hospital receives a new proposal it:
choices: Tentatively keeps its most preferred applicant among its current tentative match and the new proposer, rejecting the other.
Rejects every applicant until the final round.
Chooses the applicant with the lowest priority.
questions.answer: Tentatively keeps its most preferred applicant among its current tentative match and the new proposer, rejecting the other.
questions.explanation: Tentative acceptance can be replaced only by a more preferred proposer.

questions.prompt: Why are there at most \(n^2\) proposals in one-to-one Deferred Acceptance with \(n\) applicants and \(n\) hospitals?
choices: Each applicant proposes to each hospital at most once.
Each hospital receives at most one proposal.
The algorithm ends after exactly \(n\) rounds.
questions.answer: Each applicant proposes to each hospital at most once.
questions.explanation: There are at most \(n\cdot n\) distinct applicant-hospital proposals.

questions.prompt: Why does Deferred Acceptance produce a full matching under complete preference lists and equal numbers?
choices: If an applicant were unmatched, she must have proposed to every hospital; then every hospital would be holding someone, contradicting the existence of an unmatched hospital.
Stability directly implies fullness without assumptions.
questions.answer: If an applicant were unmatched, she must have proposed to every hospital; then every hospital would be holding someone, contradicting the existence of an unmatched hospital.
questions.explanation: The counting argument uses completeness and equal market sizes.

questions.prompt: The standard stability proof for applicant-proposing Deferred Acceptance considers a supposed blocking pair \((a,h)\). If \(a\) proposed to \(h\), then:
choices: Hospital \(h\) rejected \(a\) either immediately or later for an applicant it prefers, so \(h\) cannot prefer \(a\) to its final match.
\(a\) must have withdrawn the proposal for a worse hospital.
questions.answer: Hospital \(h\) rejected \(a\) either immediately or later for an applicant it prefers, so \(h\) cannot prefer \(a\) to its final match.
questions.explanation: Hospitals' tentative matches improve monotonically over time.

questions.prompt: If applicant \(a\) never proposed to hospital \(h\), then in the final matching:
choices: \(a\) is matched to a hospital she prefers to \(h\) (under complete lists), so \((a,h)\) cannot block.
questions.answer: \(a\) is matched to a hospital she prefers to \(h\) (under complete lists), so \((a,h)\) cannot block.
questions.explanation: Applicants propose in decreasing order of preference.

questions.prompt: Applicant-proposing Deferred Acceptance returns which stable matching?
choices: The applicant-optimal and hospital-pessimal stable matching.
The unique stable matching in every instance.
The welfare-maximizing matching by cardinal utilities.
questions.answer: The applicant-optimal and hospital-pessimal stable matching.
questions.explanation: Every applicant weakly prefers this outcome to any other stable matching.

questions.prompt: Which side has a dominant strategy to report truthfully in the standard one-to-one Deferred Acceptance mechanism?
questions.answer: The proposing side.
questions.explanation: Applicant-proposing DA is strategyproof for applicants, but not generally for hospitals.

questions.prompt: Which pairing of concept and comparator is correct?
choices: DSIC: compare truthful reporting with every misreport for each fixed \(b_{-i}\); Nash: compare unilateral deviations at one equilibrium profile.
DSIC and Nash use identical quantifiers.
Nash compares against every opponents' profile, while DSIC fixes one equilibrium profile.
questions.answer: DSIC: compare truthful reporting with every misreport for each fixed \(b_{-i}\); Nash: compare unilateral deviations at one equilibrium profile.
questions.explanation: The quantifier order makes dominant strategies much stronger.

questions.prompt: Which mechanism-design statement is correct?
choices: A welfare-maximizing allocation plus arbitrary payments need not be DSIC; payments must align incentives.
Any efficient allocation is automatically truthful.
Any critical payment makes any allocation monotone.
questions.answer: A welfare-maximizing allocation plus arbitrary payments need not be DSIC; payments must align incentives.
questions.explanation: Allocation and payment rules jointly determine incentives.

questions.prompt: Which analogy is most accurate?
choices: VCG externality payments generalize second-price payments; marginal-cost tolls internalize congestion externalities.
First-price payments and Pigouvian tolls both equal reported values.
TTC and VCG both require monetary transfers.
Nash equilibrium and DSIC are both social-welfare optima.
questions.answer: VCG externality payments generalize second-price payments; marginal-cost tolls internalize congestion externalities.
questions.explanation: Both charge an agent for harm imposed on others.

questions.prompt: Which result most directly links online learning to equilibrium?
choices: Vanishing external regret yields empirical coarse correlated equilibrium.
questions.answer: Vanishing external regret yields empirical coarse correlated equilibrium.
questions.explanation: Average regret inequalities are exactly CCE constraints.

questions.prompt: Which result most directly links equilibrium inefficiency to learning outcomes?
choices: Smoothness bounds extend from Nash equilibrium to CCE, so they apply to empirical outcomes of no-regret dynamics.
Nash's theorem implies all equilibria are efficient.
The Revelation Principle converts CCE into DSIC.
questions.answer: Smoothness bounds extend from Nash equilibrium to CCE, so they apply to empirical outcomes of no-regret dynamics.
questions.explanation: This provides welfare guarantees even when play does not converge pointwise.

questions.prompt: Which combination is generally impossible to infer from an approximation ratio alone?
choices: Truthfulness of the mechanism implementing the approximation algorithm.
A lower bound on objective value relative to optimum.
Polynomial running time if explicitly stated by the algorithm.
Feasibility of the returned allocation if proved by the algorithm.
questions.answer: Truthfulness of the mechanism implementing the approximation algorithm.
questions.explanation: Strategic monotonicity is a separate property.

questions.prompt: An agent wins under a monotone single-parameter rule at bid \(8\), loses at bid \(7\), and tie-breaking is fixed. Which payment is consistent with DSIC?
choices: Exactly her report, regardless of threshold.
A payment depending on her true value but not her report.
questions.answer: A critical value in \([7,8]\).
questions.explanation: The exact threshold may lie between the tested bids.

questions.prompt: In VCG, replacing exact welfare maximization with an arbitrary approximation algorithm while keeping VCG-style payments:
choices: Always preserves DSIC if the ratio is constant.
questions.answer: Does not generally preserve DSIC.
questions.explanation: Truthfulness of VCG relies on exact maximization over a report-independent range, or on carefully structured variants.

questions.prompt: A distribution over outcomes is a CCE but not a CE when:
choices: No player benefits from committing to a fixed deviation ex ante, but some player would benefit from a deviation conditioned on her recommendation.
It is supported on one pure Nash equilibrium.
questions.answer: No player benefits from committing to a fixed deviation ex ante, but some player would benefit from a deviation conditioned on her recommendation.
questions.explanation: CE imposes the stronger, recommendation-contingent constraints.

questions.prompt: Which statement about core stability and pairwise stability is correct?
choices: House-allocation core stability rules out blocking coalitions, while stable matching rules out blocking pairs.
They are identical definitions in all markets.
Stable matching permits blocking pairs but not coalitions.
questions.answer: House-allocation core stability rules out blocking coalitions, while stable matching rules out blocking pairs.
questions.explanation: The relevant deviation structures differ across the two models.

questions.prompt: Which theorem uses additional competition rather than distribution-specific optimization to guarantee revenue?
questions.answer: Bulow-Klemperer.
questions.explanation: One extra i.i.d. bidder lets Vickrey outperform the optimal auction with one fewer bidder.

questions.prompt: Which theorem asserts an outcome-equivalent truthful direct mechanism exists, rather than constructing efficient payments for a particular allocation rule?
questions.answer: The Revelation Principle.
questions.explanation: It is a reduction from arbitrary dominant-strategy mechanisms to direct revelation.

questions.prompt: Which theorem is primarily an implementability characterization?
questions.answer: Myerson's lemma for single-parameter environments.
questions.explanation: It characterizes exactly which allocation rules admit DSIC payments.

questions.prompt: Which property can fail for the receiving side in Deferred Acceptance even though the output is stable?
questions.answer: Strategyproofness.
questions.explanation: Stability and incentive compatibility are distinct properties.

questions.prompt: Which statement best explains why equilibrium analysis is needed when DSIC mechanisms are unavailable?
choices: Without dominant strategies, outcomes depend on strategic responses to others, so solution concepts such as Nash equilibrium predict stable behavior.
Nash equilibrium makes every game truthful.
DSIC and Nash equilibrium are identical.
questions.answer: Without dominant strategies, outcomes depend on strategic responses to others, so solution concepts such as Nash equilibrium predict stable behavior.
questions.explanation: The course shifts from rule design to the analysis of decentralized strategic interaction.

questions.prompt: Write the normalized Myerson payment identity for a single-parameter allocation rule \(x_i(b_i,b_{-i})\).
questions.explanation: Assumes \(p_i(0,b_{-i})=0\).

questions.prompt: Write the Clarke pivot payment of agent \(i\).
questions.answer: \(p_i(\mathbf b)=\max_{x\in X}\sum_{j\ne i}b_j(x)-\sum_{j\ne i}b_j(x(\mathbf b))\).
questions.explanation: Maximum welfare of others without \(i\) minus their welfare in the chosen outcome.

questions.prompt: Write bidder \(i\)'s virtual valuation.
questions.answer: \(\phi_i(v)=v-\frac{1-F_i(v)}{f_i(v)}\).

questions.prompt: Write the social cost of a nonatomic flow \(f\).
questions.answer: \(C(f)=\sum_e f_ec_e(f_e)\).

questions.prompt: Write Rosenthal's potential for an atomic congestion game.
questions.answer: \(\Phi(s)=\sum_e\sum_{j=1}^{n_e(s)}c_e(j)\).

questions.prompt: State the empirical equilibrium concept reached by vanishing external regret.
questions.answer: Coarse correlated equilibrium (CCE).

questions.prompt: State the proposal bound for one-to-one Deferred Acceptance with \(n\) applicants and \(n\) hospitals.
questions.answer: At most \(n^2\) proposals.

questions.prompt: Write the mixed expected payoff of the row player in a zero-sum matrix game.
questions.answer: \(x^TAy\).
